summary: Epic space marathon with So Many Roads and guest blues highlights
blurb: The second set erupts into a 30‑minute space sequence linking Saint Of Circumstance, He's Gone, The Wheel and So Many Roads. Guest appearances by James Cotton on Turn On Your Lovelight and Steve Miller add extra fire, while the first set delivers solid rock numbers.
review: On June 25, 1992 at Soldier Field, the Dead served up a late‑era powerhouse that still feels fresh. The opening set rolled through crisp classics – Bertha, Greatest Story Ever Told and a tight run of West L.A. Fadeaway into Brown Eyed Women – setting a solid groove. It was the second set where the magic truly unfolded: a seamless Iko Iko segue into Good Morning Little School Girl, then a monumental space trek that wove Saint Of Circumstance → He's Gone → The Wheel → So Many Roads into a nearly 30‑minute jam marathon. Jerry’s sparkling solo on “So Many Roads” and his clean, intricate fingerpicking earned unanimous praise, while Phil’s bass dropped bombs that drove the improvisations forward. Vince Welnick was on point behind the keys, and both Mickey and Bill generated a thunderous train‑horn rhythm that propelled the space stretch. Guest stars James Cotton (on Turn On Your Lovelight) and members of the Steve Miller Band added bluesy fire, capping an already electrifying night. The show’s energy, tight playing, and memorable guest moments make it one of the standout 1992 outings.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Five of six recordings are strongly positive, repeatedly praising the So Many Roads solo, the epic space sequence (Saint Of Circumstance → He's Gone → The Wheel → So Many Roads), and guest performances. Only one recording is mixed, so overall sentiment is clearly favorable.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard source with crisp guitar intro, balanced mix and no drop‑outs; captures the full show clearly and is praised as the cleanest listening experience.

key_highlights: 30‑minute space marathon linking Saint Of Circumstance → He's Gone → The Wheel → So Many Roads
"So Many Roads" solo shines with sparkling Garcia lead work
James Cotton’s guest spot on Turn On Your Lovelight and Steve Miller band energy boost the second set
Crisp soundboard recording offers crystal‑clear mix without noticeable glitches

band_performance.Jerry: Sparkling solo on So Many Roads and clean, energized playing throughout; occasional sleepy moments noted but overall strong.
band_performance.Phil: Solid bass foundation, frequently "dropping bombs" that drove jams; a few critics mention over‑driven spots during Gloria.
band_performance.Bob: Vocals praised especially on Good Morning Little School Girl; rhythm guitar shines on blues numbers with no major complaints.
band_performance.Vince: Keyboard work described as on point, vocally strong and integral to the extended space jam.
band_performance.Mickey: Percussion highlights include a memorable train‑horn segment that fuels the 30‑minute space stretch.
band_performance.Billy: Drumming delivers massive energy alongside Mickey, especially during the drum‑train and space sections.
